speaker: interviewer
text: About something in Montreal, I believe, an experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right. So this guy who was picking me up and expressing such awful things about Jewish boys on the street. Once-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was at that time at Canadian Marconi. Once he had to go on business to RCA. I didn't know that he is going there. But anyway, he's coming back, and he was furious to me. What happened? He met, at RCA Limited, the guy, the Polish guy, to whom I said that my wife was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this other-- the Polish guy, who was working with me at Canadian Marconi, comes to me furious and said, why you didn't tell me that your-- your wife is Jewish? This is terrible not telling me. He was furious because he remembered what he was saying about Jews. So he was some kind embarrassed about that, and he was furious on me. Well, I didn't answer. What I can answer? So I didn't answer him.

speaker: interviewee
text: But such-- you-- you see the picture, how the people-- He goes to the other company, meets the other man, and they don't have anything to do and talk only about some Paczynski, who who has the-- the-- the-- Jewish wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Paczynski do have a message or lesson for those listening regarding the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lesson?

speaker: interviewer
text: A lesson or a message, something you'd like to-- to say that people should remember. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh! Well, it's very, very complicated. It's not that simple. And I don't think that I could express right-- right away any lesson. Well-- any-- any-- What--

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe the most important thing that you learned?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I learned, that's another thing. That's-- I learned that nobody is safe on this world. There always somebody who is going to persecute, and it's only a sheer chance that we are in this part, or in this group, or the other group. And I like, you know, I had this priest who was teaching us in the [INAUDIBLE] school that there is a progress, a moral progress in humanity. I said, nonsense. There is no moral progress. That is, we are going down. We are getting more, more cruel, and intolerant. That's-- that's what-- what I feel. And nobody's us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, I was luck-- I had the luck that I'd been born as a Pole, and I was not circumcised and so on. But what is the difference for the person concerned to be born a Jew, or to be born a Pole, to be born a German? You see, those things are not up to us. But we suffer for I don't know for what for. And that's what my-- my impression is and my perception is. And I would say nobody should be safe, because there could-- there-- there-- always there could come time where you would be persecuted. So we are not free of persecution of the others.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I am very disappointed or very disillusioned about human beings. The human beings are specifically in the times, hard times, they-- they lose all the civilization, all these, you know, good manners or something, or-- or compassion even to other people. That's what I understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mr. Paczynski, for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Lilian. Which I consider contribute to her survival. I helped her to survive this awful, terrible war. And [SIGHS] we are married or living together from 1943 without any interruptions.

speaker: interviewee
text: What have I to say now? Yeah, we went through terrible times. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How we managed looking now, I really don't know. So I think it must be as much question of luck. But perhaps it was him, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the guardian angel, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who took care of me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, as he mentioned already we are together more than 50 years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's the story. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Stephan Visznieski, assumed name. I don't know his original name. He was from Lodz city, and he was the son of the director of the high school in Lodz. I helped him several times during the year 1942, '43. And I was in contact with him by-- by mail till the Polish uprising in Warsaw in August 1st, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- he gave it-- picture--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: --this such a picture was used to-- to be put in a document of identity. So usually he had-- he had few pictures. One of the picture went to this document and one left-- was left with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: On this picture, you can see my mother standing. Next to her is my sister. And sitting on the grass is my brother. The picture was taken in 1942 by me. This is me and my wife, Lillian. Picture was taken in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gdansk.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was pregnant.
